speaker: interviewer
text: This is April 21, 1996. Interview with Leib-- L-E-I-B-- also known as Leon-- L-E-O-N-- Dimerman-- D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N. Interview is being conducted by Mimi Singer-- M-I-M-I S-I-N-G-E-R-- in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. The interview is being conducted in English. My name is Mimi-- M-I-M-I-- Singer-- S-I-N-G-E-R. It is April 21, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Leib-- L-E-I-B-- also known as Leon-- L-E-O-N-- Dimerman-- D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N-- in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Could you tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leib Dimerman. D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been-- gone under another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In time of war, when I was 14 years, I was forced to change my name. Under Sempatia, Felix, and nationality French.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sempatia. S-E-M-P-A-T-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1926, month 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are-- you are now 69 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born in Kostopol, previous Polish-- Poland. This was a city, a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-S-T-O-P-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a community is Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a community is Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was populated-- population was 10,000 Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And near what larger city was Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yontel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Y-O-N-T-E-L, Yontel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Tichen. T-I-C-H-E-N. Tichen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana. C-H-A-N-A. Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ptecher. P-T-E-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father made a living by being a furrier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Self-employed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he always a furrier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when he was single, he was a student in [INAUDIBLE]. Now, after he married, [? it was ?] [? cut up. ?] The parents was against the marriage. And he became a-- [INAUDIBLE] [? you ?] [? learn ?] and became professional.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us who your siblings were-- your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brothers and sisters-- could you tell me who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a-- a sister in Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda. And then I have a brother Moshe, in Israel. He was [? enjoying ?] the army in Israel, all wars. And one brother is killed in Israel like a hero. His name is Meyer Dimerman. D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N. And beside the Kinneret, the forest, under his name, Meyer-- beside the Kinneret, in Israel, they give the name-- his name, Meyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- when the Arab attacked the kibbutz and he took care of the kids, the Arab came, and then the English army left to the Arab a tank. And they tried to came in with this tank in kibbutz. They would kill the kids. Then he closed up. He jumping in tank. And, with his body, he closed the tank-- the-- the visibility. And his friend put a mortar bomb, and they stopped the tank.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did he pass away?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a sister in Winnepeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood? I was attending a Polish school and a cheder. I was attending-- I was a rabbi-- I have a rabbi what teach me-- Jewish-- Judaism. And, like every child, we enjoy be a child. We was playing and studying, and this was the joyment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started off in cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started in a-- in a Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that like a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school. And then I attend a cheder. But they didn't teach Judaism. Then I attend private cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the hours of the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school was, on average, five hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- the language that you were using was Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the public school, Polish. In cheder, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you attend cheder every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cheder, we attend, I think, three times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: What hours would you attend the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school. Like, I'd say from 5:00 to 6:30. And vacation time and summertime, all days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many students would have been at the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roughly was around 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you attended Polish school up to what grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I finished grade six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after grade six?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war started. But after grade six, start the war. The Russian occupied. In 1939, they occupied Poland and our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the end of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So schooling stopped at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family celebrate holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional Jewish holiday, like average family. My mother was a very religion person. My father a little bit the opposite. He was liberal. And we was watching every holiday we used to celebrate. The real, pure, traditional Jewish way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shabbat? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat, yes. Shabbat you used to go in synagogue. Father used to go with the kids in synagogue. And came to synagogue, already dinner was ready. And we used to-- yeah, I-- I came from a traditional Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: 64 Targova. T-A-R-G-O-V-A. This is like a business street. You call this Targova.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your place of residence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You call this like 'Targova,' it's in Polish. It'd be in English 'business district.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And throughout all this time your father worked for himself as a furrier, or for somebody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Self-employed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the relationship between Jewish and non-Jewish students while you were going to the Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the small, little towns, and the smaller cities, we didn't feel the anti-Semitism so badly. Now, lately, we start to feel the anti-Semitism which the Polish-- the Polish churches, they promote. The anti-Semitism, there was preaching to the people, to the audience in church. And this we feel lately, the students in school. Sometime they make a cross with chalk-- a student in the back-- and we feel then the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice it personally, somehow? Was there anything displayed to you personally, in-- in the form of any violent acts or anything said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we didn't have it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the-- the larger community? How was the non-Jewish community to the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I can't answer this. But I was 13-- in this time I was [? of ?] Poland, I was 12 year old. I can't-- I can't tell you exactly. I never travel. Never travel in the big cities, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your community.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my community is-- like I said, it was not open. The anti-Semitism was [INAUDIBLE] not open. [INAUDIBLE]. Anti-Semitism came out not from the government channels, only from the churches.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities were you involved in, as a young boy? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like swimming, skating, skiing, ping-pong, and jumping [INAUDIBLE] in the water, fishing. This was my main sport what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like-- like every child, we are schoolboys. We enjoy winter. We were surrounded with water. We always-- lots of lakes around. And we liked skiing and skating with the-- with my friends. [INAUDIBLE] it was children, like usually like kids is enjoying it. Which the [? end ?] came-- the war, which divide us. From the happiness-- children's happiness-- and everything was disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an extended family in Kostopol? Did you have other family members in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have an aunt, and I have a grandfather, which I was very attached to them. Grandmother. And I have an uncle and some cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this on your father's side, or your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Bar mitzvah when I was 13 year old was in Kostopol, exactly before the Russian came in to us. I was-- I was 13 at the time of the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your bar mitzvah to us? Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, usually my bar mitzvah I don't remember, like-- I make bar mitzvah my kids. There was friends what you know-- my friends was invited. Maybe was around-- it's a small town. We have around 20 people. And we have a kiddish in synagogue. And when we came home, my mother prepared dinner and a little kiddish wine. And this was-- usually this is the way they make bar mitzvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you prepare to get ready for the bar mitzvah? How did you study to get ready for the bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then, this is like a regular basis. I was-- you attending-- when you're attending a rabbi, and you still you have to study Judaism. In the meantime, the rabbi's preparing for the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you prepared with the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the synagogue you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We particular didn't have it. We have only two synagogues. One synagogue was [INAUDIBLE]. It was a different kind district. And what we attending in the synagogue was the big synagogue, only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a-- what-- what nature of synagogue was it-- Orthodox? Conservative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1939, the Russians took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things change, after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came, in a short time was synagogues closed. And people was persecuted. Or they start [? believe. ?] They didn't give 'em opportunities. When the people was believers in God. And the people start to feel very [? quickly ?] and they start to acclimatize to the Russian government, and to go slowly away from their religion from believe what they believe to make a career in something, to have a better opportunity, to progress in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So religious study-- religious practice slowed down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disappeared, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any opportunity to practice the holidays, after the Russians took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm-- we practiced holidays, the Russian took over, not so openly like before. No, we practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other changes, besides the fact that religion couldn't be practiced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Financially, it became right away extreme changes an average family [? would feel. ?] Supply food, was changes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what kind of changes there were, in terms of your-- the place that you lived? Were you allowed to stay where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We didn't-- we was not rich people. We was just middle-class people, which my father was not-- was not registered to the Russian like a capitalist. He was a working man, which-- they didn't touch us, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would they have treated capitalists, instead of-- the capitalists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very bad. People [? were they ?] well-off, rich people, and some of their families, they send them to Siberia. And some of them they start to investigate, in case they was bad to the communist-- the underground communist people. And they didn't get an opportunity to get good positions. [INAUDIBLE] treat them without trust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did it seem that what they called 'capitalist' group were treated harsher than the regular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Absolutely. They didn't trust capitalists. People-- they came often a background from capitalist. They absolutely didn't trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the supply of food change? What happened, after the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russian came in was big supply. We have food. No, people were scared. Was a panic. Everybody started to prepare himself food more like he need. And the food disappeared from the store. Everything was, like, overnight disappeared. And then we was already depended on Russia have to supply. And supply came very slowly. Was lines-- lines for bread, lines for sugar, for this kind-- soap, some tobacco, you could buy free, without line. Now, the other way, some, like, jam or this kind stuff to buy, herring-- and was a lineup. And was a very bad supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about clothing? Was there enough clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very poor supply. Clothing was demand-- always a demand, Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the home in which you lived-- the setup of the home? How many rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was a very united [NON-ENGLISH]. Very united. And my aunt was living-- was a big home-- big house. And my grandfather was too big, the house. And he rent it out always, one suite, for some Polish family was renting. And my grandmother and father in the house, and I was living in the house with my parents and the kids-- my sisters and brothers. It was a big, huge home. Got a big garden with lots of trees-- different kind fruit. And we enjoy, happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the home was also rented out to a Polish couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- Polish. They have a business. They rented.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this must have been a very large house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 6,000 square feet? Which here is not so big, you know? 7,000 square feet? No, before the war, we consider this big.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family had a suite in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did-- did you have to sleep with your siblings-- in the same room, in the same bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a separate room, no. One room, we slept three brothers. And the other room-- bedroom-- two sisters, and one bedroom for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say there was also a garden attached?

speaker: interviewee
text: A garden, yeah. Big garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you grow in that garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to have cucumber, tomatoes, and different kind of salad. And the most we have trees-- apple, pears. And we used to make our own wine, for Pesach. From the apple, plums, we used to make our own wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations-- any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the organization, Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm, Betar. Since I was young. This is Jabotinsky. This is extreme to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you meet, as a-- in this organization? How would you meet with your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we have a-- a club, [? our own. ?] And a ping-pong table. And we have meetings. Once in a while, we used to go out in a [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, like [NON-ENGLISH] you call this-- like [NON-ENGLISH]. We used to go out in forest. And we used to have meetings. And the whole meeting was based in patriotism and to stretch the hand to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a Zionist youth organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many youth belonged to such an organization? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How many young people belonged to this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't remember, exactly, you know? Like, I am thinking roughly-- I think it was around-- maybe 250 kids?

speaker: interviewer
text: What ages were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ages from seven, eight, and 21, 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often would they meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met once in a week, a Saturday evening. After [YIDDISH]. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Saturdays when you met, you would do the kind of activities that you just described?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We used to enjoy. We used to-- ping-pong and the older kids used to dance and meetings. And we used to enjoy over there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would they meet? Like, what kind of a location? Where would this Betar be housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I don't remember exactly the street. I remember, like, maybe like seven, eight blocks away from me, where I live, was like a-- like a house. And big room. One huge, big room. And this was the location. We have only one location. We used to have one location.
